seoTitle: Allegro MicroSystems Products | Current Sensors, Motor Drivers | Distributor
seoDescription: Complete catalog of Allegro MicroSystems current sensors, magnetic sensors, and motor driver ICs. Technical specifications and selection guide for automotive and industrial applications.

faqs.question: What types of current sensors does Allegro offer?
faqs.answer: Allegro offers a comprehensive portfolio of Hall-effect current sensors: 1) Isolated current sensors (ACS series) - provide galvanic isolation up to 4.8kV with integrated current-carrying conductor, ideal for high-voltage applications; 2) Non-isolated current sensors - for low-voltage applications where isolation is not required; 3) High-bandwidth sensors - up to 1MHz bandwidth for AC current monitoring and fast fault detection; 4) High-precision sensors - ±1% accuracy for metering and monitoring applications; 5) Digital output sensors - I2C/SPI interface for microcontroller integration; 6) Automotive-grade sensors - AEC-Q100 qualified for -40°C to 150°C operation. Current ranges span from ±2.5A to ±200A, with various package options including SOIC-8, QFN, and custom packages for busbar mounting.
faqs.decisionGuide: Browse our Current Sensors category to find isolated, non-isolated, high-bandwidth, and automotive-grade options.

faqs.question: How do I select the right Allegro motor driver?
faqs.answer: Selecting the right Allegro motor driver depends on your motor type and application requirements: 1) Motor type - BLDC (A4931, AMT49105), stepper (A4988, DRV8825), or brushed DC (A4950); 2) Power level - integrated drivers for < 500W, pre-drivers with external MOSFETs for higher power; 3) Control interface - parallel, PWM, or SPI/I2C control; 4) Sensor requirements - sensorless BLDC control available, or Hall sensor inputs; 5) Protection features - overcurrent, thermal, undervoltage protection; 6) Automotive qualification - AEC-Q100 options for vehicle applications; 7) Advanced features - FOC, sinusoidal commutation, speed control loops. For automotive fuel pumps and fans, the AMT49105 is ideal. For industrial stepper applications, consider the A4988 or DRV8825.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use our Motor Driver selection guide or contact our FAE team with your motor type, power level, and control requirements.

faqs.question: What magnetic sensor technologies does Allegro use?
faqs.answer: Allegro employs multiple magnetic sensing technologies: 1) Hall-effect sensors - measure magnetic flux density perpendicular to chip surface, mature technology with excellent reliability; 2) TMR (Tunnel Magnetoresistance) sensors - higher sensitivity and lower power than Hall-effect, ideal for precision applications; 3) Vertical Hall technology - measure in-plane magnetic fields for 2D/3D sensing; 4) CVH (Circular Vertical Hall) - proprietary technology for high-resolution angle sensing. Sensor types include: linear position sensors (A1324), angle sensors (A1335, AAS33001), speed sensors (ATS series), and 3D magnetic sensors (A31301). Applications range from throttle position sensing to motor commutation and gear tooth detection.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use Hall-effect for general applications; TMR for high-sensitivity requirements; angle sensors for rotary position measurement.

faqs.question: What is the difference between isolated and non-isolated current sensors?
faqs.answer: Isolated current sensors (ACS series) provide galvanic isolation between the current-carrying conductor and sensor circuitry: Advantages - safe high-voltage measurement (up to 4.8kV isolation), no external isolation needed, compact integrated solution. Use for: high-voltage power supplies, motor drives, solar inverters, EV battery management. Non-isolated current sensors measure current without galvanic isolation: Advantages - lower cost, smaller size, lower power consumption. Use for: low-voltage applications (< 60V), battery-powered devices, cost-sensitive consumer products. Key selection criteria: Working voltage - use isolated if > 60V; Safety requirements - isolated for touch-safe designs; Cost - non-isolated for budget-sensitive applications. Allegro's isolated sensors integrate the current conductor, providing the simplest solution for high-voltage applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use isolated sensors for high-voltage (> 60V) applications; use non-isolated for low-voltage, cost-sensitive designs.

faqs.question: Does Allegro provide evaluation tools for their products?
faqs.answer: Yes, Allegro provides comprehensive evaluation tools: 1) Evaluation boards - dedicated PCBs for each product family with test points and connectors; 2) Demo software - GUI applications for sensor configuration and data visualization; 3) Reference designs - complete system designs for typical applications; 4) Application software - motor control firmware, sensor calibration tools; 5) Simulation models - SPICE models for circuit simulation; 6) Magnetic design tools - calculators for magnet selection and placement. Evaluation boards include: ACS evaluation kits for current sensors, motor driver demo boards with motors, LED driver evaluation systems. BeiLuo Electronics stocks popular evaluation boards and can order specific kits for your evaluation needs. Contact our FAE team for evaluation support and application guidance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact BeiLuo for evaluation board availability; request demo software and reference designs from our FAE team.

categories.name: Current Sensors
categories.description: Hall-effect current sensors with isolation, high bandwidth, and automotive qualification for EV, industrial, and power applications.
categories.longDescription: Allegro MicroSystems is the world's leading supplier of Hall-effect current sensors, offering the industry's most comprehensive portfolio of isolated and non-isolated sensing solutions. Available through authorized distributor BeiLuo, Allegro's current sensors integrate the current-carrying conductor and Hall sensor in a single package, providing galvanic isolation up to 4.8kV in compact SOIC packages. These sensors offer significant advantages over traditional shunt-based sensing: no external isolation required, < 1mΩ conductor resistance for minimal power loss, bandwidth up to 1MHz for AC monitoring, and ±1% accuracy for precision applications. The portfolio spans current ranges from ±2.5A to ±200A, with automotive-qualified options (AEC-Q100 Grade 0) for the harshest environments. Applications include EV battery management, motor drives, solar inverters, and power supplies.
series: ACS37610 - High-current busbar sensor for EV
categories.selectionGuide: Select isolated sensors for high-voltage applications; consider bandwidth for AC monitoring; choose accuracy based on metering requirements.

faqs.question: What is Hall-effect current sensing?
faqs.answer: Hall-effect current sensing uses the Hall effect to measure current without physical contact. When current flows through a conductor, it generates a magnetic field proportional to the current. A Hall sensor placed near the conductor measures this magnetic field and outputs a voltage proportional to the current. Allegro's integrated sensors combine the current conductor and Hall sensor in one package. Key advantages: galvanic isolation (no electrical connection between current path and sensor), bidirectional sensing (measures current in both directions), low power loss (conductor resistance < 1mΩ), and fast response (< 2μs). The Hall voltage is V_H = (I × B) / (n × t × e), where I is current, B is magnetic field, n is charge carrier density, t is thickness, and e is electron charge.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use Hall-effect sensors for isolated, bidirectional current measurement with fast response and low power loss.

faqs.question: How do I calculate power dissipation in current sensors?
faqs.answer: Power dissipation in Allegro current sensors comes from the conductor resistance: P = I² × R_conductor. Allegro sensors have very low conductor resistance (0.3-1.5mΩ typical), minimizing power loss. Example calculations: ACS712 (1.2mΩ): at 10A, P = 100 × 0.0012 = 0.12W; at 30A, P = 900 × 0.0012 = 1.08W. ACS37610 (0.1mΩ): at 100A, P = 10000 × 0.0001 = 1W; at 200A, P = 40000 × 0.0001 = 4W. Thermal considerations: Junction temperature Tj = Ta + (P × θja), where Ta is ambient temperature and θja is thermal resistance (typically 40-60°C/W for SOIC packages). Ensure Tj stays below maximum (usually 150°C for automotive grade). For high-current applications, provide adequate copper area on PCB for heat spreading. Some high-current sensors include thermal shutdown protection.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate P = I² × R; ensure Tj < 150°C with proper thermal design; use low-R sensors for high-current applications.

faqs.question: What bandwidth do I need for my current sensing application?
faqs.answer: Current sensor bandwidth selection depends on application requirements: DC applications (battery monitoring, power supplies): 0-10kHz bandwidth sufficient; AC line monitoring (50/60Hz): 1-10kHz bandwidth; Motor control (PWM switching): 50-100kHz to capture PWM ripple; Inverter output monitoring: 10-50kHz for fundamental frequency; Fast fault detection: > 100kHz for overcurrent protection; High-frequency power conversion: 500kHz-1MHz for GaN/SiC converters. Allegro offers sensors from DC to 1MHz bandwidth. Higher bandwidth provides faster response but may introduce more noise. For most motor control applications, 50-100kHz is adequate. For fast protection circuits, choose > 100kHz. The ACS37010 offers 1MHz bandwidth for high-frequency applications. Always verify that sensor bandwidth exceeds your signal bandwidth requirements.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 50-100kHz for motor control; > 100kHz for fast protection; 1MHz for high-frequency power conversion.

faqs.question: How do I interface Allegro current sensors with microcontrollers?
faqs.answer: Allegro current sensors offer multiple output options for microcontroller interfacing: Analog voltage output (ACS712, ACS720): Output is proportional voltage (e.g., 185mV/A for ACS712-05B), connect directly to ADC input; use voltage divider if ADC range is lower than sensor output. PWM output (ACS37800): Duty cycle proportional to current, measure with timer/capture input. Digital I2C/SPI (ACS37800, ACS37010): Direct digital interface, read current value via serial bus. Key considerations: Reference voltage - sensors may output ratiometric to Vcc or fixed reference; ADC resolution - calculate LSB size to ensure adequate current resolution; Sampling rate - sample at > 2× bandwidth for accurate capture; Filtering - add RC filter at sensor output to reduce noise (typically 1-10kHz cutoff). For high-noise environments, use differential ADC inputs or add external instrumentation amplifier.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use analog output for simple applications; digital I2C/SPI for noisy environments; PWM for isolated digital interface.

faqs.question: What accuracy can I expect from Allegro current sensors?
faqs.answer: Allegro current sensor accuracy varies by product and conditions: Total error at 25°C: ±1% to ±5% depending on device; Total error over temperature (-40°C to 150°C): ±2% to ±8%; Offset error: ±5mV to ±30mV depending on device; Sensitivity error: ±1% to ±3%; Nonlinearity: ±0.5% to ±2%. Factors affecting accuracy: Temperature - all sensors have temperature drift, use devices with internal compensation; External magnetic fields - shield or orient to minimize interference; Power supply variation - use stable Vcc for best accuracy; PCB layout - follow guidelines for optimal performance. For highest accuracy applications (energy metering), use ACS720 with ±1% total error. For general monitoring, ACS712 with ±2-3% is sufficient. Always check datasheet for specific accuracy specifications at your operating conditions.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use ±1% sensors for metering; ±2-3% for monitoring; consider temperature drift for automotive applications.

products.name: ACS712 Fully Integrated Hall-Effect Current Sensor
products.shortDescription: Economical Hall-effect current sensor with 2.1kV isolation, 5A/20A/30A ranges, and 80kHz bandwidth for AC/DC current ...
descriptionParagraphs: The ACS712 is a fully integrated Hall-effect current sensor that provides economical and precise AC or DC current sensing. The device consists of a precise, low-offset linear Hall circuit with a copper conduction path located near the surface of the die.

When current flows through the copper conduction path, the resulting magnetic field is sensed by the integrated Hall IC and converted to a proportional voltage. The device is packaged in a SOIC-8 package with internal isolation providing 2.1kV RMS dielectric strength.

The ACS712 is available in three current ranges: ±5A (185mV/A), ±20A (100mV/A), and ±30A (66mV/A). The output is ratiometric to the supply voltage and has a zero-current output of Vcc/2, allowing bidirectional current measurement.

faeReview.author: Michael Chen
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Current Sensing
faeReview.content: The ACS712 is my go-to recommendation for cost-sensitive current sensing applications. At around $1-2 in volume, it offers excellent value for basic current monitoring. The 2.1kV isolation is adequate for most low-to-medium voltage applications up to 400V working voltage. I've used it successfully in motor drives, power supplies, and battery management systems. The ratiometric output simplifies interface to 5V ADCs. Key limitations: 80kHz bandwidth limits it to lower-frequency applications - don't use for high-frequency inverter monitoring. The ±1.5% accuracy is good for monitoring but not precise enough for energy metering. Temperature drift is noticeable - expect ±3-4% over the full automotive range. For higher accuracy, step up to the ACS720. Overall, an excellent entry-level current sensor for non-critical applications.
faeReview.highlight: Excellent value at $1-2; 2.1kV isolation adequate for most apps; ratiometric output simplifies ADC interface

faqs.question: What is the maximum working voltage for ACS712?
faqs.answer: The ACS712 provides 2.1kV RMS isolation voltage between the current path and sensor circuitry. The maximum working voltage (continuous) is determined by safety agency standards and is typically 400-600V depending on pollution degree and overvoltage category. The basic insulation rating is 2.1kV RMS for 60 seconds. For reinforced insulation (touch-safe applications), derate to 600V working voltage. The isolation barrier is tested to 4.2kV peak for 60 seconds during production. For higher working voltages, consider the ACS720 with 4.8kV isolation. Always check the specific safety certifications (UL, CSA, IEC) for your application requirements and ensure compliance with local electrical safety regulations.
faqs.decisionGuide: 2.1kV isolation supports 400-600V working voltage; use ACS720 for higher voltages; check safety certifications.

faqs.question: How do I calculate the output voltage for a given current?
faqs.answer: The ACS712 output voltage is calculated as: Vout = Vcc/2 + (I × Sensitivity). For bidirectional sensing, Vcc/2 is the zero-current output (2.5V with 5V supply). For example, ACS712-05B (±5A, 185mV/A): At +5A: Vout = 2.5V + (5 × 0.185) = 3.425V; At -5A: Vout = 2.5V + (-5 × 0.185) = 1.575V; At 0A: Vout = 2.5V. The sensitivity is ratiometric to Vcc, so at 3.3V supply, sensitivity scales to 122mV/A (185 × 3.3/5). For ADC interface: Current = (Vadc - Vcc/2) / Sensitivity. Include offset calibration for best accuracy by measuring output at 0A and subtracting from readings.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use Vout = Vcc/2 + (I × Sensitivity); sensitivity is ratiometric to Vcc; calibrate offset at 0A.

faqs.question: What is the noise level of ACS712 and how do I filter it?
faqs.answer: The ACS712 has output noise of approximately 7mV RMS (21mV peak-to-peak) with 80kHz bandwidth. This corresponds to current noise of: ACS712-05B: 7mV / 185mV/A = 38mA RMS; ACS712-30A: 7mV / 66mV/A = 106mA RMS. To reduce noise, add an RC low-pass filter at the output: Cutoff frequency fc = 1 / (2πRC). For 1kHz cutoff with 1kΩ resistor: C = 1 / (2π × 1000 × 1000) = 159nF, use 150nF standard value. Trade-off: lower bandwidth means slower response. For DC measurements, use 10-100Hz cutoff. For AC line monitoring, use > 100Hz cutoff. The internal bandwidth can also be limited by adding capacitor between FILTER pin and ground (if available on variant). For lowest noise, average multiple ADC samples in software.
faqs.decisionGuide: 7mV RMS noise; add RC filter for noise reduction; trade-off between noise and bandwidth.

faqs.question: Can ACS712 measure AC current?
faqs.answer: Yes, the ACS712 can measure AC current with its 80kHz bandwidth. The output is a sine wave centered at Vcc/2 (2.5V for 5V supply). For AC measurements: Peak current = (Vpeak - 2.5V) / Sensitivity; RMS current = Peak / √2. Example: ACS712-05B measuring 3A RMS: Peak = 3 × √2 = 4.24A; Vpeak = 2.5V + (4.24 × 0.185) = 3.28V; Vmin = 2.5V - (4.24 × 0.185) = 1.72V. The output swings between 1.72V and 3.28V at the AC frequency. For microcontroller interface: Sample at > 2× AC frequency (e.g., > 100Hz for 50Hz line); Calculate RMS by averaging squared samples; Or use peak detection circuit. The 80kHz bandwidth supports AC measurements up to several kHz, making it suitable for inverter output monitoring and motor current sensing.
faqs.decisionGuide: Yes, 80kHz bandwidth supports AC; output centered at Vcc/2; sample at > 2× frequency for RMS calculation.

faqs.question: What is the temperature drift of ACS712?
faqs.answer: The ACS712 has temperature drift specifications: Sensitivity drift: ±0.5% to ±1.5% over -40°C to 150°C depending on grade; Offset drift: ±5mV to ±15mV over temperature; Total error: ±2% to ±4% over temperature range. The ECO grade (E suffix, -40°C to +85°C) has better drift characteristics than the automotive grade (K suffix, -40°C to +150°C). For highest accuracy over temperature: Use ECO grade for industrial applications; Implement temperature compensation in software using external temp sensor; Perform calibration at multiple temperatures; Use ACS720 for better temperature performance (±1% over temp). Example impact: At 100°C with 2% drift, a 10A measurement could read 9.8-10.2A. For applications requiring < 2% accuracy over temperature, consider the ACS720 or implement calibration.
faqs.decisionGuide: ±2-4% drift over temperature; use ECO grade for better specs; implement calibration for highest accuracy.

products.name: ACS37610 High-Current Busbar Current Sensor
products.shortDescription: High-precision current sensor for busbar mounting with ±200A range, 1MHz bandwidth, and 4.8kV isolation for EV applic...
descriptionParagraphs: The ACS37610 is a high-precision, high-bandwidth current sensor designed for busbar mounting in high-current applications such as electric vehicle battery management and high-power motor drives. The sensor features Allegro's proprietary Hall-effect technology with advanced signal processing.

With a sensing range up to ±200A and 1MHz bandwidth, the ACS37610 can accurately measure both DC and high-frequency AC currents. The device provides 4.8kV isolation voltage, making it suitable for high-voltage EV battery packs up to 800V.

The sensor offers ±1% accuracy at 25°C with integrated temperature compensation maintaining accuracy across the automotive temperature range. The differential Hall sensing architecture rejects external magnetic fields, providing reliable measurements in noisy electromagnetic environments.
features: Differential Hall sensing for noise immunity
Busbar mounting for low thermal resistance

faeReview.author: David Park
faeReview.title: Principal FAE - Automotive Systems
faeReview.content: The ACS37610 is my top recommendation for high-current EV applications. The 1MHz bandwidth is exceptional - I've used it to monitor SiC inverter switching with excellent results. The busbar mounting is a game-changer for thermal management; with proper copper area, we've measured < 50°C temperature rise at 200A continuous. The differential sensing really works - we saw < 1% error even with 1000A/cm external fields nearby. The 4.8kV isolation gives plenty of margin for 800V EV systems. One consideration is the custom footprint - you'll need to design your busbar specifically for this sensor. Also, the differential output requires a differential ADC or instrumentation amplifier, adding some complexity. But for high-current, high-voltage applications, the performance is unmatched. The AEC-Q100 Grade 0 qualification is essential for automotive. Overall, the best high-current sensor I've worked with.
faeReview.highlight: 1MHz bandwidth excellent for SiC inverters; busbar mounting provides superior thermal performance; differential sensing rejects external fields

faqs.question: How do I mount the ACS37610 on a busbar?
faqs.answer: The ACS37610 requires custom mechanical mounting on a busbar: 1) Design busbar with flat area for sensor mounting; 2) Ensure clean, flat surface for good thermal contact; 3) Use thermal interface material (TIM) between sensor and busbar; 4) Apply uniform mounting pressure using screws or clips; 5) Torque screws to specification (typically 0.5-1.0 Nm); 6) Ensure no air gaps for optimal heat transfer. Thermal design: The sensor dissipates P = I² × R_conductor (typically < 0.1mΩ). At 200A, P = 4W. With thermal resistance of 5°C/W (typical with good mounting), temperature rise is 20°C. Maintain ambient + rise < 150°C. PCB connections: Route sense and power connections away from high-current busbar. Use twisted pairs for differential output. Keep high-current paths short and wide. Allegro provides mechanical drawings and thermal simulation guidelines.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use TIM for thermal contact; apply uniform pressure; ensure good thermal design for high-current operation.

faqs.question: What is the differential output of ACS37610?
faqs.answer: The ACS37610 provides differential analog output for noise immunity: Vout_diff = Vout+ - Vout- = Sensitivity × I. The outputs are centered around common-mode voltage (typically Vcc/2). For ±200A range with 10mV/A sensitivity: At +200A: Vout+ = 2.5V + 1.0V = 3.5V, Vout- = 2.5V - 1.0V = 1.5V, Vdiff = 2.0V; At 0A: Vout+ = Vout- = 2.5V, Vdiff = 0V; At -200A: Vout+ = 1.5V, Vout- = 3.5V, Vdiff = -2.0V. Interface options: Differential ADC - connect Vout+ and Vout- directly to differential inputs; Single-ended ADC - use instrumentation amplifier (e.g., AD620) to convert differential to single-ended; Or sample both outputs separately and subtract in software. Differential signaling provides: Rejection of common-mode noise; Better EMI immunity; Longer cable runs possible. For best performance, route differential traces as twisted pair or closely coupled differential traces.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use differential ADC or instrumentation amp; route as twisted pair; provides excellent noise immunity.

faqs.question: What is the overcurrent detection feature?
faqs.answer: The ACS37610 includes a fast overcurrent detection (OCD) output: Function - digital output goes low when current exceeds programmed threshold; Response time - < 1μs from overcurrent to OCD output; Threshold - programmable via external resistor or digital interface; Hysteresis - prevents chatter near threshold; Use cases - fast fault protection for IGBTs/MOSFETs, short-circuit detection, current limiting. Configuration: Set threshold resistor Rth = (Vcc × Rint) / (Vth - Voffset), where Rint is internal reference, Vth is desired threshold voltage. Or program digitally via I2C/SPI if available. Connection: Connect OCD output to microcontroller interrupt or directly to gate driver shutdown. The OCD operates independently of the analog output, providing redundant protection. Example: For 150A overcurrent threshold with 10mV/A sensitivity: Vth = 150A × 10mV/A = 1.5V. Configure accordingly.
faqs.decisionGuide: Program threshold based on protection requirements; < 1μs response for fast protection; use for gate driver shutdown.

faqs.question: Can ACS37610 measure high-frequency ripple current?
faqs.answer: Yes, the ACS37610's 1MHz bandwidth enables high-frequency ripple current measurement: Capability - accurately measure ripple up to ~500kHz (Nyquist criterion); Applications - SiC/GaN inverter output monitoring, switching power supply ripple, motor drive current ripple; Measurement - the sensor captures both DC component and AC ripple; Output - analog output follows ripple waveform in real-time; For digital systems, sample at > 2× ripple frequency. Example: SiC inverter switching at 100kHz: The sensor captures the 100kHz switching ripple superimposed on DC current; With 1MHz bandwidth, amplitude error is minimal (< 3dB). For analysis: Use oscilloscope to view ripple waveform; Calculate ripple amplitude and RMS; Verify inverter operation and filter performance. The high bandwidth is a key advantage over lower-bandwidth sensors (80-120kHz) that would attenuate high-frequency components.
faqs.decisionGuide: 1MHz bandwidth captures ripple to 500kHz; ideal for SiC/GaN inverter monitoring; sample at > 2× ripple frequency.

faqs.question: What external magnetic field rejection does ACS37610 provide?
faqs.answer: The ACS37610 uses differential Hall sensing for excellent external field rejection: Architecture - two Hall elements sense magnetic field from opposite sides of conductor; Common-mode rejection - external fields affect both sensors equally and cancel out; Rejection ratio - typically > 40dB (100:1) for uniform external fields; Performance - < 1% error with 1000A/cm external fields. Test conditions: Place sensor near high-current conductor (e.g., adjacent phase in 3-phase system); External field from adjacent conductor can be 100-500 Gauss; Differential sensing reduces error to < 1% of full scale. Limitations: Non-uniform fields (e.g., nearby magnet) may not cancel completely; Best practice - maintain distance from strong magnetic sources; Use magnetic shielding if necessary for extreme environments. The differential architecture is a significant advantage over single-element sensors for applications in high-field environments like motor drives and power converters.
faqs.decisionGuide: Differential sensing provides > 40dB external field rejection; < 1% error in high-field environments; ideal for motor drives.

faqs.question: What is the accuracy advantage of ACS720 over ACS712?
faqs.answer: The ACS720 provides ±1% total error at 25°C compared to ACS712's ±1.5%. This 0.5% improvement is significant for precision applications: Energy metering - 0.5% accuracy improvement directly impacts billing accuracy and revenue; Motor control - better torque control and efficiency optimization; Power monitoring - more accurate power calculations and load balancing. Over temperature, the ACS720 maintains ±2% error vs ACS712's ±3-4%, providing better stability in automotive and industrial environments. The improved accuracy comes from better Hall element design, advanced temperature compensation, and tighter factory calibration. For applications where accuracy is critical, the ACS720's premium is justified by performance gains.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use ACS720 for ±1% accuracy requirements; ACS712 for cost-sensitive applications where ±1.5% is acceptable.

faqs.question: How does the 4.8kV isolation benefit high-voltage applications?
faqs.answer: The ACS720's 4.8kV RMS isolation provides significant advantages for high-voltage applications: Safety margin - 4.8kV provides 6:1 safety margin for 800V EV systems (6× working voltage); Regulatory compliance - meets reinforced insulation requirements for touch-safe designs; Working voltage - supports up to 1600V working voltage for basic insulation, 800V for reinforced insulation; Transient protection - withstands voltage spikes and transients common in power electronics. Comparison to ACS712 (2.1kV): ACS720 supports higher working voltages; Meets stricter safety standards; Suitable for 800V EV systems; Better long-term reliability. Applications benefiting: EV battery management (400V/800V), solar inverters (1000V DC), motor drives (480V AC), industrial power supplies.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use ACS720 for 400V-800V systems requiring 4.8kV isolation; ACS712 sufficient for < 400V applications.

faqs.question: What is the thermal performance of ACS720?
faqs.answer: The ACS720 thermal performance characteristics: Conductor resistance - 0.85mΩ typical, generating P = I² × R heat; Temperature rise - depends on current and PCB thermal design; SOIC-16 package - larger than SOIC-8, providing better heat dissipation. Thermal calculations: At 20A: P = 400 × 0.00085 = 0.34W; At 40A: P = 1600 × 0.00085 = 1.36W. With typical θja of 40°C/W for SOIC-16 on standard PCB: At 20A: ΔT = 0.34 × 40 = 13.6°C; At 40A: ΔT = 1.36 × 40 = 54.4°C. Thermal design recommendations: Use large copper areas (≥ 1 oz, ≥ 100mm²) for heat spreading; Add thermal vias to inner layers; Consider forced air cooling for continuous high-current operation; Monitor junction temperature to stay below 150°C. The ACS720's better thermal performance vs ACS712 enables higher continuous current operation.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use adequate copper area for heat dissipation; monitor temperature at max current; consider forced air for continuous operation.

faqs.question: How do I select the right current range for ACS720?
faqs.answer: ACS720 current range selection guidelines: Available ranges - ±10A (200mV/A), ±20A (100mV/A), ±40A (50mV/A). Selection criteria: Maximum current - choose range 1.5-2× maximum expected current; Resolution - lower current ranges provide better resolution (mA/bit); Accuracy - all ranges have ±1% total error; Bandwidth - 120kHz for all ranges. Examples: 5A motor - select ±10A range for best resolution; 15A power supply - select ±20A range with margin; 30A inverter - select ±40A range. Resolution comparison at 12-bit ADC (5V): ±10A: 20A/4096 = 4.9mA resolution; ±20A: 40A/4096 = 9.8mA resolution; ±40A: 80A/4096 = 19.5mA resolution. Trade-offs: Smaller range = better resolution but less headroom; Larger range = more headroom but lower resolution. For dynamic loads, ensure peak currents stay within range.
faqs.decisionGuide: Select range 1.5-2× max current; use ±10A for best resolution; ±40A for high-current applications.

faqs.question: What applications benefit most from ACS720's features?
faqs.answer: Applications benefiting from ACS720's high accuracy and isolation: Energy metering - ±1% accuracy meets revenue-grade requirements; precise power measurement for billing; Solar inverters - 4.8kV isolation for 1000V DC systems; accurate MPPT power monitoring; Motor control - precise torque control with ±1% accuracy; fast overcurrent protection; EV charging - isolated sensing for 400V/800V systems; accurate charge monitoring; Industrial drives - robust operation in noisy environments; accurate power monitoring; Power supplies - precise current limiting and monitoring; feedback for control loops. The combination of accuracy, isolation, and bandwidth makes ACS720 ideal for applications where measurement precision directly impacts system performance or revenue. The AEC-Q100 qualification also makes it suitable for automotive applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use ACS720 for metering, solar, motor control, EV charging where accuracy and isolation are critical.

products.name: ACS37800 Power Monitoring IC with Isolated Current and Voltage Sensing
products.shortDescription: Digital power monitoring IC with isolated current sensing and voltage measurement for AC/DC power monitoring applicat...
descriptionParagraphs: The ACS37800 is a fully integrated power monitoring IC that combines isolated current sensing, voltage measurement, and power calculation in a single device. The integrated Hall-effect current sensor provides galvanic isolation up to 4.8kV, while the internal voltage sensing enables direct connection to high-voltage rails.

The device features a digital I2C/SPI interface that provides instantaneous current, voltage, and power readings. Integrated diagnostics detect fault conditions including overcurrent, overvoltage, and undervoltage conditions. The ACS37800 eliminates the need for external shunt resistors, isolation amplifiers, and voltage dividers, significantly reducing BOM cost and PCB area.

With a current range of ±30A to ±90A and voltage measurement up to 600V, the ACS37800 is ideal for AC/DC power supplies, solar inverters, and industrial motor drives. The 1.2MHz bandwidth enables accurate measurement of high-frequency switching waveforms.

faeReview.author: Jennifer Liu
faeReview.title: FAE - Power Systems
faeReview.content: The ACS37800 is a game-changer for power monitoring applications. The integration of current and voltage sensing with digital output eliminates multiple discrete components - no shunt resistors, no isolation amplifiers, no voltage dividers. This reduces BOM cost by 30-40% and PCB area by 50%. The 1.2MHz bandwidth is excellent for switching power supply monitoring - I've used it to measure PFC stage efficiency with great results. The integrated diagnostics are valuable for detecting fault conditions without microcontroller intervention. The I2C interface makes it easy to integrate with modern microcontrollers. One consideration is the fixed current range - you'll need to select the appropriate variant for your application. Also, the digital interface requires more software development than analog sensors. But for power monitoring applications, the ACS37800 offers unmatched integration and performance.
faeReview.highlight: 30-40% BOM cost reduction; 50% PCB area savings; integrated diagnostics; excellent for PFC monitoring

alternativeParts.useCase: Applications requiring > 90A or preferring analog output

faqs.question: How does ACS37800 calculate power?
faqs.answer: The ACS37800 integrates current and voltage measurement to calculate real-time power: Current measurement - Hall-effect sensor measures load current with 4.8kV isolation; Voltage measurement - high-impedance resistive divider measures bus voltage; Power calculation - internal multiplier computes P = V × I; Energy accumulation - integrated energy counter tracks total energy consumption. The device provides instantaneous current, voltage, power, and energy readings via I2C/SPI interface. Power measurement accuracy is ±2-3% when combining current and voltage errors. The 1.2MHz bandwidth captures high-frequency power variations in switching supplies. For AC applications, the device can measure instantaneous power and calculate RMS values. The digital interface eliminates ADC errors present in analog sensor systems.
faqs.decisionGuide: ACS37800 provides integrated power calculation; accuracy ±2-3%; digital interface eliminates ADC errors.

faqs.question: What is the I2C interface speed of ACS37800?
faqs.answer: The ACS37800 supports I2C interface up to 1MHz (Fast Mode Plus): Standard Mode - up to 100kHz; Fast Mode - up to 400kHz; Fast Mode Plus - up to 1MHz. The device supports clock stretching and has internal registers for configuration and data reading. Register map includes: Current reading (16-bit signed); Voltage reading (16-bit); Power reading (16-bit); Energy counter (32-bit); Configuration registers; Status and fault registers. Reading all parameters takes approximately 100μs at 400kHz. The device also supports SPI interface up to 8MHz for faster data transfer. For power monitoring applications, 400kHz I2C is typically sufficient for 1-10kHz update rates. The digital interface includes CRC protection for data integrity.
faqs.decisionGuide: Supports up to 1MHz I2C; 100μs read time at 400kHz; SPI available up to 8MHz for faster transfer.

faqs.question: How do I use ACS37800 for AC power monitoring?
faqs.answer: AC power monitoring with ACS37800: Connection - connect current sensor in series with AC line or neutral; connect voltage sense to AC line with appropriate safety considerations; ensure 4.8kV isolation between high-voltage and low-voltage sides. Measurement - the device measures instantaneous current and voltage; power is calculated as P = V × I for each sample; positive power indicates power consumption, negative indicates generation. RMS calculation - for AC measurements, calculate RMS by sampling over multiple cycles; I_rms = sqrt(sum(I²)/N); V_rms = sqrt(sum(V²)/N); P_avg = sum(P)/N. Power factor - calculate as PF = P_avg / (V_rms × I_rms). Update rate - sample at > 2× AC frequency (e.g., > 100Hz for 50Hz line); average multiple cycles for stable readings. The 1.2MHz bandwidth captures AC waveform details accurately.
faqs.decisionGuide: Sample at > 2× AC frequency; calculate RMS over multiple cycles; use for power factor measurement.

faqs.question: What diagnostics does ACS37800 provide?
faqs.answer: The ACS37800 includes comprehensive diagnostic features: Overcurrent detection - programmable threshold with fast response; Overvoltage detection - monitors bus voltage for overvoltage conditions; Undervoltage detection - detects low voltage conditions; Fault flags - status register indicates fault conditions; Overtemperature detection - monitors die temperature. Diagnostic configuration: Program thresholds via configuration registers; Enable/disable individual diagnostics; Configure fault response (flag only or automatic actions); Read fault status via I2C/SPI. Use cases: Overcurrent protection - fast shutdown of power stage; Overvoltage protection - prevent damage to downstream circuits; System monitoring - track operating conditions for predictive maintenance. The diagnostics operate independently of the main measurement path, providing redundant protection. Fault outputs can be connected to microcontroller interrupts or directly to system shutdown circuits.
faqs.decisionGuide: Programmable thresholds for overcurrent, overvoltage, undervoltage; independent fault flags; use for protection and monitoring.

faqs.question: How does ACS37800 compare to discrete solutions?
faqs.answer: ACS37800 vs discrete power monitoring solutions: Discrete solution components - shunt resistor (1-5mΩ); isolation amplifier (e.g., AMC1300); voltage divider (high-resistance); ADC (microcontroller or external); total BOM: 8-12 components. ACS37800 solution - single IC with all functions integrated; total BOM: 1 IC + bypass caps. Comparison: PCB area - ACS37800 uses 50% less board space; Cost - ACS37800 reduces BOM cost by 30-40%; Accuracy - comparable when using high-quality discretes; Isolation - both provide galvanic isolation; Bandwidth - ACS37800 1.2MHz vs typical 100-200kHz for isolation amps; Development time - ACS37800 reduces design time significantly. Trade-offs: Current range - discrete solution can be customized; Flexibility - discrete allows component optimization; Availability - single IC vs multiple components. For most power monitoring applications, the ACS37800 offers compelling integration benefits.
faqs.decisionGuide: ACS37800 reduces BOM cost 30-40%, PCB area 50%; comparable accuracy; faster development vs discrete solutions.

categories.name: Motor Drivers
categories.description: Integrated motor drivers for BLDC, stepper, and brushed DC motors with advanced control features and automotive qualification.
categories.longDescription: Allegro MicroSystems offers a comprehensive portfolio of motor driver ICs for brushless DC (BLDC), stepper, and brushed DC motors. Available through authorized distributor BeiLuo, these drivers provide complete motor control solutions with integrated power stages, protection features, and advanced control algorithms. The portfolio includes integrated drivers for motors up to 500W and pre-drivers for external MOSFET configurations supporting higher power levels. Key features include sensorless BLDC control, sinusoidal commutation, microstepping for stepper motors, and comprehensive fault protection. Automotive-qualified options (AEC-Q100) support the demanding requirements of vehicle applications including electric power steering, fuel pumps, and HVAC systems. Allegro's motor drivers reduce system complexity and BOM cost while providing reliable, efficient motor control.
series: AMT49105 - Automotive BLDC driver with sensorless control
A4988 - Stepper motor driver with microstepping
categories.selectionGuide: Select integrated drivers for < 500W motors; use pre-drivers with external MOSFETs for higher power; consider automotive qualification for vehicle applications.

faqs.question: What motor types do Allegro drivers support?
faqs.answer: Allegro motor drivers support three main motor types: Brushless DC (BLDC) Motors - Three-phase permanent magnet motors requiring electronic commutation. Allegro offers integrated drivers (AMT49105) and pre-drivers (A4931) with sensorless and sensored control options. Features include trapezoidal and sinusoidal commutation, speed control loops, and FOC support. Stepper Motors - Two-phase motors moving in discrete steps. Allegro's A4988 and DRV8825 provide microstepping control with up to 1/32 step resolution, reducing vibration and improving smoothness. Brushed DC Motors - Simple two-wire motors controlled by H-bridge drivers like A4950. Support PWM speed control, direction reversal, and braking. Each motor type has specific drivers optimized for their control requirements, with options ranging from basic open-loop control to advanced closed-loop systems with sensor feedback.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use AMT49105 for BLDC motors; A4988 for stepper motors; A4950 for brushed DC motors.

faqs.question: What is the difference between integrated drivers and pre-drivers?
faqs.answer: Integrated motor drivers include the control logic and power MOSFETs in one package: Advantages - complete solution, compact size, simplified design, lower BOM count. Limitations - power limited by package thermal performance (typically < 500W), fixed MOSFET characteristics. Use for: low to medium power applications, space-constrained designs, cost-sensitive products. Pre-drivers provide control logic and gate drive for external MOSFETs: Advantages - flexible power scaling, custom MOSFET selection, better thermal management, higher power capability (> 1kW possible). Limitations - requires external MOSFETs and more design effort, larger PCB area. Use for: high-power applications, custom performance requirements, thermal-critical designs. Allegro's AMT49105 is an integrated driver up to 200W, while A4931 is a pre-driver for external MOSFET configurations supporting higher power.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use integrated drivers for < 500W; pre-drivers with external MOSFETs for > 500W or custom requirements.

faqs.question: How do I size a motor driver for my application?
faqs.answer: Motor driver sizing involves several key parameters: Continuous current - driver must handle motor's rated current continuously. Include 50% margin: I_driver = 1.5 × I_motor_rated. Peak current - driver must handle startup and overload currents. Motors can draw 3-5× rated current during startup. Voltage rating - driver supply voltage must exceed motor voltage with margin. Use V_driver = 1.2 × V_motor. Power dissipation - calculate driver losses: P = I² × Rds(on) for integrated drivers. Ensure thermal design can handle dissipation. Protection features - ensure driver has adequate overcurrent, thermal, and undervoltage protection. Example: 100W motor at 24V: I_rated = 4.2A; Select driver for 6-8A continuous, 15-20A peak; Voltage rating > 30V; Verify thermal performance at max current. Allegro datasheets provide thermal derating curves for proper sizing.
faqs.decisionGuide: Size for 1.5× continuous current, 3-5× peak current; verify thermal performance; include voltage margin.

faqs.question: What control interfaces do Allegro motor drivers support?
faqs.answer: Allegro motor drivers support multiple control interfaces: Parallel control - direct logic inputs for direction, enable, and speed (PWM). Simplest interface, suitable for basic applications. Example: A4950 uses IN1/IN2 inputs for direction control. Step/Direction - standard interface for stepper motors. Step input triggers movement, direction input sets rotation direction. A4988 uses this interface with microstepping configuration pins. PWM speed control - duty cycle controls motor speed. Enable input for on/off control. SPI/I2C - serial interface for configuration and control. Allows software setup of parameters like current limit, decay mode, and fault monitoring. AMT49105 supports SPI for advanced configuration. Sensor inputs - Hall sensor inputs for BLDC commutation (A4931). Analog inputs - for current feedback and closed-loop control. The choice depends on application complexity, microcontroller capabilities, and required features.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use parallel/PWM for simple applications; SPI/I2C for advanced configuration; Step/Direction for stepper motors.

faqs.question: What protection features are included in Allegro motor drivers?
faqs.answer: Allegro motor drivers include comprehensive protection features: Overcurrent Protection (OCP) - monitors motor current and shuts down if exceeding limit. Fast response (< 1μs) protects power devices during short circuits. Programmable threshold in some devices. Overtemperature Protection (OTP) - monitors die temperature and shuts down when exceeding safe limit (typically 150-165°C). Automatic restart when temperature decreases. Prevents thermal damage. Undervoltage Lockout (UVLO) - disables driver if supply voltage drops below minimum. Prevents erratic operation during brownout or startup. Overvoltage Protection (OVP) - protects against supply transients and regenerative braking spikes. Cross-Conduction Prevention - ensures high-side and low-side switches never conduct simultaneously, preventing shoot-through. Fault Reporting - status outputs or SPI registers indicate fault conditions for system monitoring. These protections ensure reliable operation and prevent equipment damage under fault conditions.
faqs.decisionGuide: All Allegro drivers include comprehensive protection; OCP < 1μs essential for power device protection.

products.name: AMT49105 Automotive BLDC Motor Driver
products.shortDescription: Automotive BLDC motor driver with integrated sensorless control, sinusoidal commutation, and 200W output capability.
descriptionParagraphs: The AMT49105 is a highly integrated three-phase BLDC motor driver designed for automotive applications. The device integrates sensorless control algorithms, sinusoidal commutation, and power MOSFETs in a compact package, providing a complete motor control solution.

With integrated sensorless control, the AMT49105 eliminates the need for Hall sensors in many applications, reducing system cost and improving reliability. The sinusoidal commutation algorithm provides smooth, quiet operation compared to trapezoidal control, reducing torque ripple and audible noise.

The device supports motor power up to 200W with supply voltages from 6V to 50V. Comprehensive protection features include overcurrent, overtemperature, undervoltage, and short-circuit protection. The AEC-Q100 Grade 0 qualification ensures reliable operation from -40°C to 150°C.
features: Speed control loop with PI compensation

faeReview.author: Michael Chen
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Automotive Motor Control
faeReview.content: The AMT49105 is my go-to solution for automotive BLDC applications. The integrated sensorless control eliminates Hall sensors, which are a common failure point in automotive environments. The sinusoidal commutation provides noticeably smoother and quieter operation compared to trapezoidal control - customers often comment on the reduced noise. I've used this device in numerous fuel pump and cooling fan applications with excellent results. The 200W power capability handles most automotive auxiliary motors. The SPI interface allows fine-tuning of control parameters for optimal performance with specific motors. Key design considerations: ensure adequate PCB copper area for thermal management; the QFN package requires good soldering process; implement proper EMI filtering for automotive compliance. The comprehensive protection features have saved many designs from field failures. For production, I recommend implementing the fault reporting through SPI to enable predictive maintenance.

faqs.question: What is sensorless BLDC control and when should I use it?
faqs.answer: Sensorless BLDC control uses back-EMF sensing to determine rotor position instead of Hall sensors: Principle - As the motor rotates, the permanent magnets induce voltage (back-EMF) in the stator windings. By monitoring the back-EMF zero-crossings, the controller determines when to commutate. Advantages - Lower cost (no Hall sensors), higher reliability (fewer components), simpler wiring, smaller size. Limitations - Difficult startup (unknown position), poor low-speed performance, requires minimum speed for reliable detection. Use sensorless when: Cost is critical; High reliability required; Motor runs at moderate to high speeds (> 10% of max); Load is predictable. Use sensored when: Precise position control needed; Low-speed operation required; High startup torque needed; Load varies significantly. The AMT49105 supports both modes - configure for sensorless or connect Hall sensors for sensored operation.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use sensorless for cost-sensitive, high-reliability applications at moderate speeds; use sensored for low-speed or precision applications.

faqs.question: How does sinusoidal commutation compare to trapezoidal?
faqs.answer: Sinusoidal vs trapezoidal commutation for BLDC motors: Trapezoidal (six-step) - Simple switching pattern; 60° conduction angle per phase; Produces torque ripple at 6× electrical frequency; Higher audible noise; Good efficiency at high speeds; Easier to implement. Sinusoidal - Smooth current waveforms; 180° conduction with sinusoidal shape; Minimal torque ripple; Quiet operation; Better efficiency at low to medium speeds; More complex control algorithm. Performance comparison: Torque ripple - Trapezoidal: 13-15%; Sinusoidal: < 5%. Efficiency - Sinusoidal 5-10% better at low speeds, similar at high speeds. Noise - Sinusoidal significantly quieter. The AMT49105 implements sinusoidal commutation with sensorless control, providing smooth, quiet operation ideal for automotive applications where NVH (noise, vibration, harshness) is critical.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use sinusoidal for quiet, smooth operation; trapezoidal for simple, cost-sensitive designs.

faqs.question: What is the maximum PWM frequency supported?
faqs.answer: The motor driver supports PWM frequencies up to 20kHz. Higher frequencies reduce audible noise but increase switching losses. For most applications, 10-20kHz provides a good balance between noise and efficiency. The device includes programmable dead-time to prevent shoot-through at high switching frequencies.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 10-20kHz PWM for optimal balance of noise and efficiency.

faqs.question: How do I implement thermal management for the motor driver?
faqs.answer: Thermal management guidelines: Calculate power dissipation P = I² × Rds(on) for conduction losses plus switching losses. Use PCB copper area (≥ 1 oz) for heat spreading. Add thermal vias under the package to conduct heat to inner layers. For high-power applications, consider external heatsinks or forced air cooling. Monitor temperature with the device's thermal warning output. The device includes thermal shutdown at 165°C for protection.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate dissipation, use copper area for heat spreading, add thermal vias, monitor temperature.

faqs.question: What protection features are built into the driver?
faqs.answer: The motor driver includes comprehensive protection: Overcurrent protection (OCP) with programmable threshold monitors motor current and shuts down during faults. Overtemperature protection (OTP) shuts down at 165°C junction temperature. Undervoltage lockout (UVLO) prevents operation below minimum supply voltage. Cross-conduction prevention ensures both high-side and low-side switches are never on simultaneously. Short-circuit protection responds in < 1μs to protect power devices.
faqs.decisionGuide: All protections are automatic; configure OCP threshold for your application.

products.name: A4988 Stepper Motor Driver
products.shortDescription: Complete microstepping motor driver with translator and easy operation for bipolar stepper motors up to 2A per coil.
descriptionParagraphs: The A4988 is a complete microstepping motor driver with built-in translator for easy operation. It is designed to operate bipolar stepper motors in full-step, half-step, quarter-step, eighth-step, and sixteenth-step modes, with output drive capability up to 35V and ±2A.

The translator is the key to the easy implementation of the A4988. By simply inputting one pulse on the STEP input, the motor will take one step (full, half, quarter, etc.). The direction is selected by a simple logic level on the DIR input. No phase sequence tables or high-frequency control lines are required.

The A4988 includes a fixed off-time current regulator that operates in slow or mixed decay modes. The current decay mode is determined by the state of the MS1 and MS2 pins. Mixed decay reduces audible motor noise and vibration compared to slow decay.

The built-in translator eliminates the need for complex phase sequencing - just send pulses and the motor moves. I've used it in countless 3D printer and CNC designs with excellent reliability. The A4988 is cost-effective and widely available, making it ideal for consumer and light industrial applications. For higher current requirements, consider the DRV8825 which is pin-compatible but supports up to 2.5A.

faqs.question: How do I set the current limit on A4988?
faqs.answer: The A4988 current limit is set using the on-board potentiometer and VREF pin: Calculation - I_chop = VREF / (8 × Rs), where Rs is the sense resistor (typically 0.05Ω or 0.1Ω). For default 0.05Ω sense resistors: I_chop = VREF / 0.4. To set 1A current: VREF = 1A × 0.4 = 0.4V. Procedure: 1) Power on with motor disconnected; 2) Measure voltage between VREF pin and GND; 3) Adjust potentiometer to desired VREF; 4) Reconnect motor and test. Important notes: Set current to motor's rated current or slightly below; Higher current increases torque but also heating; Always use heatsink or cooling for currents > 1A; Current chopping may cause audible noise at certain frequencies. The potentiometer provides approximately 0-2V range, supporting currents up to 2A with 0.05Ω sense resistors.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate VREF = I × 0.4 (for 0.05Ω Rs); set to motor rated current; use cooling for > 1A.

faqs.question: What is the difference between slow and mixed decay modes?
faqs.answer: Slow and mixed decay modes affect current regulation and motor performance: Slow decay - Current recirculates through one low-side MOSFET and the motor winding; Slower current decay, better current regulation at low speeds; Lower ripple current, less motor heating; Can cause instability at high speeds. Mixed decay - Current decays through both low-side and high-side paths; Faster current decay, better high-speed performance; Higher ripple current, slightly more heating; Recommended for most applications. The A4988 automatically selects decay mode based on step rate: Slow decay at low speeds for stability; Mixed decay at high speeds for performance. For most applications, use the automatic mode. If experiencing resonance or missed steps at certain speeds, try forcing mixed decay mode.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use automatic mode for most applications; force mixed decay if experiencing resonance or missed steps.

faqs.answer: The motor driver supports PWM frequencies up to 20kHz. Higher frequencies reduce audible noise but increase switching losses. For most applications, 10-20kHz provides a good balance between noise and efficiency. The device includes programmable dead-time to prevent shoot-through at high switching frequencies.

products.name: A4950 Full-Bridge DC Motor Driver
products.shortDescription: Full-bridge motor driver with PWM current control for brushed DC motors up to 40V and 3.5A.

The integrated charge pump eliminates the need for external capacitors while providing sufficient gate drive voltage for low RDS(on) operation. The A4950 can drive motors with supply voltages up to 40V and continuous currents up to 3.5A, with peak currents up to 5A.

The internal PWM current control is a standout feature - it automatically regulates motor current without requiring the microcontroller to generate PWM signals. This simplifies software development and reduces EMI by using the driver's internal oscillator. The integrated charge pump is another plus, eliminating external capacitors and reducing BOM cost. The diagnostic output is valuable for automotive systems - it can detect open-load, short-circuit, and overtemperature conditions. The AEC-Q100 qualification is essential for automotive applications. Overall, a reliable and easy-to-use DC motor driver.

faqs.question: How does the internal PWM current control work?
faqs.answer: The A4950's internal PWM current control automatically regulates motor current: Current sensing - internal sense resistor monitors motor current; Comparator - compares sensed current to reference; PWM generation - internal oscillator generates PWM when current exceeds limit; H-bridge control - PWM modulates bridge to maintain average current. Operation: When current < limit: both low-side switches on, current increases; When current > limit: switches alternate, current decreases; Average current maintained at programmed level. Benefits: No microcontroller PWM required; fixed frequency reduces EMI; automatic current regulation; protects motor from overcurrent. Configuration: Set current limit via sense resistor; PWM frequency fixed at ~25kHz (inaudible); Current ripple typically 10-20% of limit. The internal control loop operates independently, freeing the microcontroller for other tasks.
faqs.decisionGuide: Internal PWM eliminates MCU PWM generation; fixed 25kHz frequency; automatic current regulation.

faqs.question: What is the diagnostic output used for?
faqs.answer: The A4950 diagnostic output (DIAG) provides fault indication: Fault types detected - Overcurrent (short circuit); Overtemperature (thermal shutdown); Undervoltage (UVLO active); Open load (no motor connected). Output behavior: Normal operation: DIAG pin high (pulled up); Fault condition: DIAG pin low (pulled down); Multiple faults: Pin stays low until all faults cleared. Connection options: Connect to microcontroller GPIO with pull-up; Or connect to LED with current limit resistor; Or use for system shutdown circuit. Software implementation: Poll DIAG pin periodically; Or configure as interrupt for immediate response; Read pin state to detect faults; Take appropriate action (stop motor, log fault, etc.). The diagnostic feature is essential for automotive applications requiring fault detection and reporting.
faqs.decisionGuide: DIAG pin goes low during faults; connect to MCU or LED; essential for automotive fault detection.

faqs.question: How do I set the current limit for my motor?
faqs.answer: Setting the A4950 current limit: Formula - I_limit = V_ref / (8 × R_sense); Where V_ref is internal 1V reference, R_sense is external sense resistor. Calculation example: For 2A current limit: R_sense = 1V / (8 × 2A) = 0.0625Ω = 62.5mΩ; Use standard 62mΩ resistor. For 3A current limit: R_sense = 1V / (8 × 3A) = 0.0417Ω = 41.7mΩ; Use standard 43mΩ or 39mΩ resistor. Resistor selection: Use low-inductance resistors (metal film or chip resistors); Power rating: P = I² × R (e.g., 3A × 3A × 0.043Ω = 0.39W, use 1W resistor); Tolerance: 1% or better for accurate current limit. Verification: Measure voltage across sense resistor at max current; Should be V = I × R_sense; Adjust resistor if needed. Always set current limit 20-30% above motor rated current for margin.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use I_limit = V_ref / (8 × R_sense); set 20-30% above motor rating; use 1% tolerance resistors.

faqs.question: What thermal management is required for A4950?
faqs.answer: A4950 thermal management guidelines: Power dissipation calculation - P = I² × Rds(on) for conduction losses; Rds(on) = 350mΩ total (high+low side); At 3A: P = 9 × 0.35 = 3.15W; At 2A: P = 4 × 0.35 = 1.4W. Temperature rise: T_rise = P × θja; θja depends on PCB design (typically 40-60°C/W for SOIC-8); At 3A with 50°C/W: T_rise = 3.15 × 50 = 157°C (exceeds limit). Thermal design: Use large copper area (≥ 1 oz) for heat spreading; Add thermal vias under package to inner layers; Consider exposed pad package for better thermal performance; Add heatsink for continuous high-current operation. Operating limits: Maximum junction temperature: 150°C; Recommended maximum: 125°C for reliability; Thermal shutdown activates at 165°C. For continuous 3A operation, forced air cooling or heatsink may be required.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate P = I² × Rds(on); use copper area and vias; consider heatsink for > 2.5A continuous.

faqs.question: Can A4950 drive motors in both directions?
faqs.answer: Yes, the A4950 full-bridge configuration supports bidirectional motor control: Forward direction - IN1 high, IN2 low: current flows from OUT1 to OUT2; motor rotates clockwise; Reverse direction - IN1 low, IN2 high: current flows from OUT2 to OUT1; motor rotates counter-clockwise; Brake/coast - both inputs high or both low: motor braking or coasting. Control logic: IN1 | IN2 | Function; 0 | 0 | Coast (both outputs high-impedance); 1 | 0 | Forward (current OUT1→OUT2); 0 | 1 | Reverse (current OUT2→OUT1); 1 | 1 | Brake (both outputs low). PWM speed control: Apply PWM to enable pin for speed control; Or alternate between forward and coast for PWM control; Current regulation active in all modes. The full-bridge topology provides complete motor control with direction, speed, and braking capability using just two logic inputs.
faqs.decisionGuide: Full-bridge supports forward, reverse, brake, coast; use IN1/IN2 inputs for direction control.

products.name: A4931 Three-Phase BLDC Pre-Driver
products.shortDescription: Three-phase BLDC motor pre-driver with synchronous rectification for high-power motor applications.
descriptionParagraphs: The A4931 is a three-phase brushless DC (BLDC) motor pre-driver designed for high-power applications requiring external MOSFETs. The device provides gate drive signals for six external N-channel MOSFETs in a three-phase bridge configuration.

The A4931 features synchronous rectification mode that reduces power dissipation by using the low-side MOSFETs as synchronous rectifiers instead of body diodes. This improves efficiency, especially at high currents. The device supports both sensored (Hall effect) and sensorless commutation modes.

Advanced protection features include adjustable dead-time to prevent shoot-through, overcurrent protection with programmable threshold, undervoltage lockout, and overtemperature protection. The device includes a charge pump for driving high-side MOSFETs and bootstrap capacitors for the gate drive supply.
features: Three-phase gate drive for external MOSFETs

faeReview.author: Robert Kim
faeReview.title: Principal FAE - Motor Systems
faeReview.content: The A4931 is an excellent choice for high-power BLDC applications where integrated drivers can't handle the current requirements. As a pre-driver, it gives you the flexibility to select MOSFETs matched to your specific power and efficiency requirements. I've used this part in automotive fuel pump applications up to 500W with excellent results. The synchronous rectification feature is a significant advantage - it can improve efficiency by 5-10% compared to diode rectification, especially at high currents. The sensorless commutation mode works well for applications where Hall sensors aren't practical, though sensored mode provides better low-speed torque. Key design considerations: Select MOSFETs with appropriate RDS(on) for your current requirements; carefully design the gate drive circuit for fast switching; implement proper dead-time to prevent shoot-through; ensure adequate thermal design for the external MOSFETs. The programmable overcurrent protection is valuable for protecting the power stage. Overall, a flexible and reliable solution for high-power BLDC control.
faeReview.highlight: Flexible MOSFET selection for any power level; synchronous rectification improves efficiency; supports sensorless operation

alternativeParts.useCase: Applications up to 500W where integrated driver is sufficient
